note: Verifies (a) that a WASI guest has zero ambient authority, and (b) that Wasmtime 46.0.0 lets a FilePerms::READ file be modified through a hard link into a FilePerms::all() preopen, while 46.0.1 refuses the link. Pass/fail on operation outcome plus the protected file's final CONTENT — the content is what settles it, because the write in step 3 'succeeds' on both versions and means opposite things.

host_preopens.ro: DirPerms::all() + FilePerms::READ  — directory may be reorganized, file CONTENT is read-only

precondition.why_this_config: If `ro` were given DirPerms::READ only, the link's source side would already be refused and the CVE could not appear at all. DirPerms::all() + FilePerms::READ ('you may tidy the directory, you may not rewrite the files') is the precise vulnerable precondition — and a realistic one.

behaviors_verified.claim: A WASI guest starts with NO filesystem authority. Without a preopen, a file that exists on disk is not merely unreadable — it does not exist from the guest's point of view.
behaviors_verified.evidence: observed-output.txt → EXP-A, second transcript (`wasmtime run guest_a.wasm`, no --dir)
behaviors_verified.value: allowed.txt → No such file or directory (os error 44), even though the file is present on the host

behaviors_verified.claim: With one directory handed over (`--dir sandbox::/`), reads inside it succeed, a relative escape `../` is refused as a permission violation, and an absolute host path is invisible.
behaviors_verified.value: allowed.txt = "allowed-content-123"; ../secret.txt → os error 63 (Operation not permitted); /etc/hosts → os error 44

behaviors_verified.claim: On 46.0.0 a read-only-content file can be rewritten by hard-linking it into a writable preopen: the direct write-open is refused, but the link succeeds and the write through the new name modifies the protected original.
behaviors_verified.value: step1 BLOCKED (os error 63) · step2 LINKED · step3 WROTE · step4 ro/secret.txt = "MODIFIED-VIA-LINK"

behaviors_verified.claim: On 46.0.1 the same guest, same host, same preopen config: the hard link itself is refused and the protected original is untouched.
behaviors_verified.value: step1 BLOCKED · step2 BLOCKED (os error 63) · step3 WROTE · step4 ro/secret.txt = "SECRET-readonly-original"

behaviors_verified.claim: Step 3 reports WROTE on BOTH versions. On 46.0.0 that write lands on the protected inode; on 46.0.1 `rw/leaked` is a separate new file. The outcome of the operation says nothing — only the protected file's final content does.
behaviors_verified.evidence: observed-output.txt → EXP-C, step 3 vs step 4 across both transcripts
behaviors_verified.value: identical step-3 line, opposite step-4 content
behaviors_verified.result: verified (this is the methodological point of the run)

cited_not_measured.claim: The vulnerable code validated directory-mutation permission only and did not check that file permissions matched; rename was affected by the same path; symlinks were not affected.
The wasmtime CLI is unaffected because it always sets FilePerms::all() on every preopen.
cited_not_measured.source: GHSA-4ch3-9j33-3pmj advisory. Consistent with the CLI's own --dir help text observed in this run, which offers no read-only flag — but 'the CLI always grants all perms internally' was not independently read out of the source here.
GHSA-4ch3-9j33-3pmj advisory. Only the 46.0.0 vs 46.0.1 pair was executed; the other branches are cited, not tested.

cited_not_measured.claim: WASI 0.3.0 was ratified 2026-06-11 and Wasmtime 46.0.0 (2026-06-22) is the first release with WASI 0.3.0 / component-model-async on by default.
cited_not_measured.source: Bytecode Alliance WASI 0.3 announcement + Wasmtime v46.0.0 release notes

explicitly_not_verified: preview2 / component-model guests. The run is wasm32-wasip1 (preview1) only. The advisory's scope covers the shared wasmtime-wasi FilePerms check, but that was not executed here.
The other affected branches (24.x / 36.x / 45.x). Only the 46.0.0 ↔ 46.0.1 boundary was run.
Non-Windows hosts. Hard-link and rename semantics vary by OS; these transcripts are Windows 11.
rename() as a second bypass vector. The advisory names it; this run exercised hard_link only.
